posts.text:
US bond market volatility is surging:

The ICE BofA MOVE Index is up to 95 points, the highest since June 2025.

The index, also called the “VIX of bonds,” measures the yield volatility of 2-year, 5-year, 10-year, and 30-year Treasuries.

The gauge has risen +37 points, or +64% over the last 6 weeks, the fastest jump since "Liberation Day."

This month alone, the 10Y Treasury yield rose +35 basis points to 4.28%, near the highest since August.

At the same time, the 30Y yield soared +30 basis points, to 4.90%, also near a 7-month high.

Markets are pricing-in higher inflation from the Iran war.
posts.author: KobeissiLetter

posts.text:
I watched the beautiful peafowl flying in @Rainmaker1973 video and my mind went to the mistreatment in zoo and other resorts in that keep the birds for tourist profits.

Wclipping (flight feather trimming) in birds, including peacocks/peafowl, based on avian welfare sources, expert opinions, and reports from bird keepers/vets result in emotional damage. 

These emotional/psychological impacts stem from the loss of flight as a core natural behavior for escape, exploration, exercise, and coping, often leading to frustration when birds can't respond instinctively to their environment.

Frustration: Birds repeatedly attempt to fly but fail, leading to agitation, pacing, screaming, or acting out (e.g., biting or excessive vocalizing). Many sources describe this as a direct result of blocked natural drives.

Stress / Chronic Anxiety: Flight is birds' primary way to escape perceived threats or discomfort; without it, they feel vulnerable and trapped, showing elevated stress (e.g., higher stress hormones, hesitation, phobic behaviors, or shaking).

Fear / Reduced Confidence: Clipped birds often become more timid, hesitant to explore, or lose trust in their abilities/environment sometimes described as "broken spirit" or increased nervousness around people/threats.

Depression / Lethargy: Owners and experts frequently report birds seeming "depressed," with reduced activity, boredom, withdrawal, or a sense of helplessness (linked to "learned helplessness" where they stop trying to engage actively).

Anger / Irritability: Some describe outbursts of aggression or self-directed behaviors (e.g., feather-plucking as an outlet for built-up frustration and inability to cope healthily).

These effects are more pronounced in species reliant on flight (like parrots or peafowl) and in less enriched environments, though individual variation exist, some birds adapt better in large, social zoo settings. Welfare advocates highlight that even if physical pain isn't involved, these emotional tolls make clipping a significant concern for long-term well-being.

I’ve liked zoo’s as a young child. I would look into the eyes of many of the inhabitants or just watch the body language and empathize with the caged cats or physically manipulated birds. (I was fine with the snakes behind glass)
posts.author: BambroughKevin
